policy_statement: Develop a publicly accessible community energy map identifying opportunities for distributed energy resources and microgrids to improve energy resiliency.
mechanism_description: Develop a publicly accessible community energy map to identify where distributed energy resources and microgrids can be deployed, supporting improved energy resiliency.
primary_causal_pathway: Climate hazard + grid stressor → ↑ system preparedness/planning → deployment of DERs/microgrids → ↓ exposure to outages + ↑ service continuity
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: A city creates and publishes a community energy map that identifies priority locations, load profiles, critical facilities, and grid constraints where DERs and microgrids are most valuable.  
2) Mediating process: Better spatial/technical information reduces planning and coordination barriers, enabling investment and siting of islandable microgrids and distributed generation/storage that can operate during grid failures.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Improved energy resilience—fewer/shorter outages and reduced impacts on health, safety, and critical services during climate-driven extreme events.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced vulnerability to climate-amplified grid disruptions by enabling targeted DER/microgrid deployment that maintains critical electricity service during extreme events.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Climate hazards increase outage risk; mapping enables DER/microgrid deployment that reduces exposure to outages and maintains service—an adaptation/resilience pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While DERs/microgrids can reduce emissions if they displace fossil generation, the mapping policy is explicitly framed around resiliency and does not inherently ensure decarbonization; the most general, always-applicable effect across instances is reduced disruption impacts.  
3) Edge cases applied: If microgrids are predominantly diesel/gas-backed, mitigation could be negative, but resilience benefits remain; if mapping is paired with requirements for renewables/storage, mitigation becomes a strong co-benefit but not the dominant guaranteed effect of the mechanism as stated.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy that creates an energy resilience map to guide DER/microgrid deployment is principally about improving preparedness and continuity of power under hazards, which is adaptation.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the mapped deployments are renewables-based (mitigation co-benefit) or fossil-based, and which hazards are targeted, must be determined at the individual policy/program implementation level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the mechanism were redefined to require renewable DERs/storage (or explicitly target emissions reductions), the dominant pathway could shift toward Mitigation; as written (resilience mapping enabling microgrids), Adaptation is the most consistent primary category.
key_indicators: “Develop a publicly accessible community energy map”; “identifies opportunities”; “deploying distributed energy resources and microgrids”; “improve energy resiliency”
co_benefits: Potential air-quality/public-health improvements from cleaner distributed generation; potential cost savings and grid efficiency benefits from optimized DER siting
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be interpreted as an information-disclosure instrument because it is “publicly accessible,” but the primary function is planning/priority-setting to guide future DER/microgrid deployment.
